abstract: Traditional animal models and static cell cultures remain the standard for investigating mercury ion (Hg(II)) transport, yet they frequently fail to replicate the complex human intestinal microenvironment or provide real-time physiological monitoring. Consequently, the precise mechanisms governing Hg(II) absorption in the human gut remain poorly understood. In this study, we developed a gut-on-a-chip platform integrated with transepithelial electrical resistance (TEER) and electrochemical sensors to simulate the formation of the intestinal barrier and monitor Hg(II) transport in situ. By applying fluid shear stress (0.02 dyne/cm²) and cyclic mechanical strain (1%, 0.15 Hz), we successfully recreated a dynamic cellular microenvironment. Using this biomimetic system, we simultaneously tracked electrochemical signatures of Hg(II) absorption and TEER-measured physical damage across varying mercury concentrations. Our findings reveal that increasing tensile strain from 1% to 5% led to a 23.59% rise in Hg(II) absorption, a phenomenon correlated with the upregulated expression of the mechanosensitive ion channel Piezo1 and the metal transporter DMT1. This integrated platform offers a robust tool for studying heavy metal toxicity and the mechanobiology of intestinal transport.
